background: A manufacturer of automobile batteries claims that the average life span of its grade A battery is 60 months. The guarantee on this brand is for just 36 months. The standard deviation of the life span is known to be 10 months, and the frequency distribution of the life-length data is known to be mound-shaped.

task.query: Approximately what percentage of the manufacturer's grade A batteries will last more than 50 months, assuming the manufacturer's claim is true?
task.answer: It is easy to see in Figure 2.22 that the percentage of batteries lasting more than 50 months is approximately 34% (between 50 and 60 months) plus 50% (greater than 60 months). Thus, approximately 84% of the batteries should have life length exceeding 50 months.

task.query: Approximately what percentage of the manufacturer's batteries will last less than 40 months, assuming the manufacturer's claim is true?
task.answer: The percentage of batteries that last less than 40 months can also be easily determined from Figure 2.22. Approximately 2.5% of the batteries should fail prior to 40 months, assuming the manufacturer's claim is true.

task.query: Suppose your battery lasts 37 months. What could you infer about the manufacturer's claim?
task.answer: If you are so unfortunate that your grade A battery fails at 37 months, you can make one of two inferences: either your battery was one of the approximately 2.5% that fail prior to 40 months, or something about the manufacturer's claim is not true. Because the chances are so small that a battery fails before 40 months, you would have good reason to have serious doubts about the manufacturer's claim. A mean smaller than 60 months and/or a standard deviation longer than 10 months would both increase the likelihood of failure prior to 40 months.

meta_info.caption: Making a Statistical Inference-Car Battery Guarantee
meta_info.chapter: Methods for Describing Sets of Data
meta_info.section: 2.5 Using the Mean and Standard Deviation to Describe Data
